summary: A long-lived spring butterfly that overwinters as an adult, breeds on buckthorns, and stays common in Sweden even though the GBIF map is dominated by observer-rich countries rather than a simple abundance gradient.

fieldRecord.habitatNote: The linked photos show an overwintered adult resting low in dry leaf litter and woody edge vegetation during an early-spring flight period.
fieldRecord.observationNote: EXIF-backed photo record from the linked image set. Running scripts/build_site_data.py will fill date, time and coordinates from the matching photos.
fieldRecord.locationLabel: Håga, Uppsala kommun, Uppsala län, Sverige

media.caption: Closed wings show why resting brimstones disappear so easily into last year's leaves.
Close view of the underside pattern and the small orange discal spot on the wing.
The thin pale wing and leaf-tip shape remain visible even in a worn spring individual.

hero.lede: Brimstone is one of the clearest Swedish examples of how a common butterfly can still tell a strong ecological story. It overwinters as an adult, depends on buckthorns for breeding, and can appear in spring long before many people expect butterflies to be active at all. This species is worth checking repeatedly through spring because overwintered adults can appear on the first warm days, while later in the season the same species shifts into a different habitat-use pattern.
hero.shortMorphology: Large pale-yellow to greenish-yellow butterfly with sharply pointed leaf-like wings; resting adults show a strong dead-leaf effect and a small orange discal spot on each wing.

taxonomy.source: GBIF species match with Catalogue of Life checklistKey
taxonomy.resolvedAgainstDataset: Catalogue of Life checklist via GBIF species/match?checklistKey=7ddf754f-d193-4cc9-b351-99906754a03b

distribution.globalSummary: Gonepteryx rhamni is a widespread western-Palearctic brimstone. The filtered GBIF layer used here is best read as an observation surface, not a native-range map, and it is strongly inflated by countries with dense butterfly-recording communities.
distribution.swedenSummary: In Sweden the species is resident, reproducing and assessed as LC in 2025. Swedish GBIF coverage is substantial, but the global map is still visually dominated by the UK, the Netherlands and other countries with strong observer networks.

notes: The map shows aggregated GBIF occurrence density rather than individual points.
Filters are restricted to HUMAN_OBSERVATION and MACHINE_OBSERVATION records from year 2000 onwards and to records with occurrenceStatus = PRESENT.
The webpage renders this layer as a hex summary map.

distribution.samplingBiasNote: GBIF density here reflects recorder effort, digitization, and the strength of local butterfly-reporting systems as much as biological distribution. The UK and the Netherlands dominate the filtered layer, so dense hexes should not be read as a direct abundance gradient and sparse regions should not be treated as absence.

internationalContext.summary: Internationally, Brimstone is a useful species because it is common enough to be familiar but structured enough to be ecologically informative: breeding depends on buckthorns, adults overwinter, and some populations use different parts of the landscape across the year. During this refresh, I confirmed a Europe-scale IUCN assessment of Least Concern from 2010 but did not locate a separate global IUCN Red List assessment to cite with the same confidence.

status.system: European Red List of Butterflies / IUCN Europe
status.label: Least Concern
status.note: No separate global IUCN assessment was confirmed during this refresh.

ecology: Larvae are closely tied to buckthorns, especially Frangula alnus and Rhamnus cathartica, even though the adult butterfly ranges widely through the landscape.
The species overwinters as an adult, which helps explain why it can be one of the first conspicuous butterflies on warm spring days.
Field studies in mountain systems show seasonal elevational movement, with summer and return movements responding to both climate and resource availability.

Adult feeding behaviour responds to floral scent cues rather than to a single nectar source, while reproductive phenology in Sweden is shaped by the long adult lifespan and the overwintering strategy.
The pale-yellow wing colour and resting leaf mimicry are not just aesthetic features; they are part of how the butterfly remains conspicuous in flight but cryptic at rest.
threats: Local breeding can still be limited where host shrubs are removed, fragmented or disconnected from sheltering habitat.
Landscape simplification can separate breeding sites from summer refuges and overwintering cover even when adults remain easy to see in spring.
Climate change may relax thermal limits in some regions, but seasonal movement still depends on resource availability and habitat continuity.

phenology.northernEurope: Adults overwinter and can fly on mild late-winter or spring days; breeding follows host-plant flush and later-season adults move toward overwintering cover.
phenology.note: The species is unusually good for watching how weather shifts first-flight timing between years.

swedishContext.summary: In Sweden, citronfjäril is a classic spring signal rather than a red-list concern. Artfakta and the local 2025 Swedish Red List data make that clear: the species is treated as accepted, resident and reproducing, with a national category of LC and an increasing population. That combination makes it a strong visitor-facing species because it links a familiar spring sighting to host-plant dependence, overwintering biology and observer bias in open biodiversity data.

redList2025.documentation: Populationen är ökande. De skattade värdena som bedömningen baserar sig på ligger alla inom intervallet för kategorin Livskraftig (LC).

keyFacts: Artfakta/Dyntaxa taxon 201051 treats Gonepteryx rhamni as an accepted species and citronfjäril as the recommended Swedish name.
The Swedish Red List 2025 data classify the species as LC and state that the population is increasing.
The local red-list data also treat the species as bofast och reproducerande, with immigration history spontan.
Because adults overwinter, citronfjäril is one of the most useful Swedish butterflies for revisiting very early in spring.

artfakta.note: Use Artfakta as the main Swedish species-information source when refreshing this page locally.

literature.title: Oviposition strategies in Pieridae butterflies and the role of an egg-killing plant trait therein
literature.whyRelevant: Useful for breeding ecology because it includes Gonepteryx rhamni in a modern analysis of oviposition strategy on natural host plants.

literature.title: Climate conditions and resource availability drive return elevational migrations in a single-brooded insect
literature.whyRelevant: Best source here for seasonal habitat partitioning, return elevational migration and climate-resource interactions.

literature.title: Colors and pterin pigmentation of pierid butterfly wings
literature.whyRelevant: Supports the colour-structure context behind the species' bright wing appearance.

literature.title: Early Male Emergence and Reproductive Phenology of the Adult Overwintering Butterfly Gonepteryx rhamni in Sweden
literature.whyRelevant: Directly useful for Swedish phenology because it focuses on reproductive timing in an adult-overwintering population.

literature.title: Foraging responses in the butterflies Inachis io, Aglais urticae (Nymphalidae), and Gonepteryx rhamni (Pieridae) to floral scents
literature.whyRelevant: Useful for adult nectar-foraging ecology because it tests behavioural responses to floral scent cues.
